title: Phase coherence control of a programmable high-Tc superconductor created by light
summary: The quest for superconductivity created by light extends for more than half a century, yet direct evidence of a true zero-resistance state -whose macroscopic quantum phase coherence is both created and controlled by light -- has remained elusive. Here we report for the first time on a complex but robust light-programmable superconducting (LiPS) state at an aluminium-silicon heterojunction that is created and fully controlled with femtosecond laser pulses. The superconducting critical temperatures -- ranging from 1.8-8.5 K, can be increased or erased at will by the application of tailored pulse sequences. At low temperatures the LiPS state shows features characteristic of a Berezinski-Kosterlitz-Thouless topological transition, but another distinct state appears at temperatures above 2 K, which shows clear signatures of quantum phase disorder. In the presence of a magnetic field we observe behaviour characteristic of vortex pinning and creep consistent with the 2-dimensional (2D) nature of the phase coherent system. The origin of the LiPS effect is attributed to light pulse control of the Moire-like superlattice of misfit dislocations (MDs) that naturally occur as a result of discommensurations between Al and Si lattices at the interface, and is clearly observable by high-resolution electron microscopy. We show how light pulses can be used to control the superlattice periodicity and highlight the appearance of topologically protected soliton-like kinks along the dislocation lines, important for imparting metastability to the system. The demonstration of LiPS opens a route to the design of metastable long-range phase coherent superconducting states, leading to light-engineering of quantum circuits, local gap tuning in quantum processors and novel devices utilizing switchable superconductivity.

AI.tldr: First demonstration of a light-programmable superconducting (LiPS) state at an Al-Si heterojunction created and controlled by femtosecond laser pulses, with critical temperatures tunable from 1.8-8.5 K, showing Berezinski-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition characteristics and vortex pinning behavior in a 2D phase coherent system.
AI.motivation: The long-standing quest for light-induced superconductivity has lacked direct evidence of a true zero-resistance state with macroscopic quantum phase coherence created and controlled by light.
AI.method: Femtosecond laser pulses were used to create and control the superconducting state at an aluminium-silicon heterojunction. High-resolution electron microscopy was employed to characterize the Moire-like superlattice of misfit dislocations at the interface.
AI.result: The LiPS state exhibits tunable critical temperatures (1.8-8.5 K) that can be increased or erased by tailored pulse sequences. At low temperatures, Berezinski-Kosterlitz-Thouless topological transition features are observed. Above 2 K, quantum phase disorder signatures appear. Magnetic field experiments reveal vortex pinning and creep consistent with 2D nature.
AI.conclusion: Light pulse control of the Moire-like superlattice of misfit dislocations creates metastable long-range phase coherent superconducting states. This enables light-engineering of quantum circuits, local gap tuning in quantum processors, and novel switchable superconductivity devices.

title: Spin fluctuation-mediated unconventional superconductivity in ThFeAsN from first-principles
summary: Superconducting (SC) pairing mechanism, origin of high $T_c$ and symmetry of SC order parameter in Fe-based superconductors are among the important unsolved problems in condensed matter and materials physics. We study the SC properties of ThFeAsN, a Fe-based high $T_c$ superconductor, by {\it ab initio} superconducting density functional theory calculations with electron-phonon coupling, screened static and dynamic electron-electron Coulomb repulsion and spin fluctuation (SF) mediated pair-interaction fully taken into account. Our calculations reveal that ThFeAsN is a SF-mediated multiband superconductor with the calculated $T_c$ of 22.4 K and the $d_{xy}$-wave SC order parameter with different signs on different Fermi surface sheets, in consistent with experiments. We also present distinct SC properties such as quasiparticle density of states and ultrasonic attenuation coefficient which can be immediately verified by experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper studies ThFeAsN, an Fe-based high Tc superconductor, using ab initio superconducting density functional theory. The calculations reveal it is a spin fluctuation (SF)-mediated multiband superconductor with a calculated Tc of 22.4 K and dxy-wave order parameter, consistent with experiments.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to solve important unsolved problems in condensed matter physics: the superconducting pairing mechanism, origin of high Tc, and symmetry of SC order parameter in Fe-based superconductors. ThFeAsN was chosen as a model system for investigation.
AI.method: The study employs ab initio superconducting density functional theory calculations that fully incorporate: (1) electron-phonon coupling, (2) screened static electron-electron Coulomb repulsion, (3) dynamic electron-electron Coulomb repulsion, and (4) spin fluctuation (SF) mediated pair-interaction.
AI.result: The calculated Tc is 22.4 K. The SC order parameter has dxy-wave symmetry with different signs on different Fermi surface sheets. Additional predicted properties include quasiparticle density of states and ultrasonic attenuation coefficient.
AI.conclusion: ThFeAsN is confirmed to be a spin fluctuation-mediated multiband superconductor. The dxy-wave order parameter with sign changes between different Fermi surface sheets is consistent with experimental observations. The predicted quasiparticle density of states and ultrasonic attenuation coefficient provide experimental verification targets.

title: Imaging and characterization of spontaneous vortices in a proximity-induced superconductor
summary: Observation of spontaneous symmetry breaking is crucial to our understanding of a continuous second-order phase transition from a disordered system into an ordered one, which often leads to the formation of topological defects. In the case of superconductors, such topological defects are identified as quantized vortices. However, their geometrical characteristics have not been well investigated and understood yet. For the imaging of spontaneous vortices, we employ a proximity-coupled superconducting nanocomposite. Here we show from scanning superconducting quantum interference device microscope measurements that vortices with different polarities, size and shapes are observed stochastically especially under near zero-field conditions. The field distributions of the spontaneous vortices are more extended and intricate than those of the field induced Abrikosov vortices, yielding longer penetration depths ranging from ~1 to ~10 micrometer. The spontaneous vortices are created during cooling as a result of the competition between Josephson coupling and thermal phase fluctuation, both of which are inherently present in this proximity-coupled system. The morphology of the vortices most likely imprints the information that is frozen at the time of vortex formation, providing insights into the local phase differences that are present in the early stage of the phase transition.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates spontaneous vortices in a proximity-coupled superconducting nanocomposite using scanning superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) microscopy. The researchers observed vortices with varying polarities, sizes, and shapes under near zero-field conditions, with field distributions more extended than traditional Abrikosov vortices and penetration depths ranging from 1-10 micrometers.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand spontaneous symmetry breaking and topological defects in continuous second-order phase transitions in superconductors. While quantized vortices are known to form during phase transitions, their geometrical characteristics have not been well investigated. The researchers aim to image and characterize spontaneous vortices that form during the cooling process.
AI.method: The researchers used a proximity-coupled superconducting nanocomposite and employed scanning superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) microscope measurements to image the spontaneous vortices.
AI.result: The study found that vortices with different polarities, sizes, and shapes appear stochastically, especially under near zero-field conditions. The field distributions of these spontaneous vortices are more extended and intricate compared to field-induced Abrikosov vortices, with penetration depths ranging from approximately 1 to 10 micrometers. The vortices are created during cooling due to competition between Josephson coupling and thermal phase fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: The morphology of the spontaneous vortices likely encodes information frozen at the time of vortex formation, providing insights into local phase differences present in the early stages of the phase transition. This offers a new window into understanding phase transition dynamics in superconductors.

title: $c$-axis strain tuning of superconductivity and symmetric elastoresistivity in CsV$_3$Sb$_5$
summary: The kagome metal CsV$_{3}$Sb$_{5}$ hosts an intriguing interplay between charge-density-wave (CDW) order and superconductivity that is highly sensitive to lattice distortions. However, determining the specific roles of the in-plane ($A_{1g,1}$) and out-of-plane ($A_{1g,2}$) symmetric strain channels has been hindered by their intrinsic mixing in conventional piezo-based experiments. Here, we combine in-plane uniaxial strain with direct $c$-axis compression to independently access and disentangle these symmetry-resolved responses in CsV$_{3}$Sb$_{5}$. We reveal that $c$-axis compression drives a massive, linear enhancement of the superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$) alongside a suppression of $T_{\rm CDW}$. The tuning efficiency of this out-of-plane deformation acts with an opposite sign and far exceeds that of in-plane strain, demonstrating that $c$-axis lattice control dictates the phase competition. Furthermore, by isolating the pure elastoresistivity coefficients, we find that the out-of-plane cross-coupling coefficient ($m_{13}$) is comparable in magnitude but opposite in sign to the in-plane response ($m_{11}+m_{12}$). Unlike the sharply peaked in-plane response, $m_{13}$ exhibits a distinct, order-parameter-like onset across the CDW transition. Our results establish that out-of-plane lattice control plays a dominant role in tuning the intertwined states in CsV$_{3}$Sb$_{5}$ and provide a general pathway for resolving strain-coupled electronic responses in layered quantum materials.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the interplay between charge-density-wave (CDW) order and superconductivity in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 by independently controlling in-plane and out-of-plane strain. Using combined uniaxial strain and c-axis compression, researchers demonstrate that out-of-plane lattice control dominates the phase competition, with c-axis compression strongly enhancing Tc while suppressing TCDW.
AI.motivation: Previous studies on CsV3Sb5 were limited by intrinsic mixing of in-plane (A1g,1) and out-of-plane (A1g,2) strain channels in conventional piezo-based experiments, preventing clear determination of their specific roles in tuning CDW order and superconductivity.
AI.method: The researchers combined in-plane uniaxial strain with direct c-axis compression to independently access and disentangle the symmetry-resolved responses in CsV3Sb5. This approach allowed isolation of pure elastoresistivity coefficients to compare in-plane and out-of-plane responses.
AI.result: C-axis compression drives a massive linear enhancement of superconducting transition temperature (Tc) alongside suppression of TCDW. The out-of-plane tuning efficiency acts with opposite sign and far exceeds that of in-plane strain. The out-of-plane cross-coupling coefficient (m13) is comparable in magnitude but opposite in sign to the in-plane response (m11+m12). Unlike the sharply peaked in-plane response, m13 exhibits a distinct order-parameter-like onset across the CDW transition.
AI.conclusion: Out-of-plane lattice control plays a dominant role in tuning the intertwined states in CsV3Sb5. This work provides a general pathway for resolving strain-coupled electronic responses in layered quantum materials.

title: Interlayer sliding direction as a symmetry selector in altermagnetic bilayer Fe2WS4: Switchable anomalous Hall and anomalous valley Hall effects
summary: Altermagnets combine compensated collinear magnetic order with momentum-dependent spin splitting, offering a promising platform for coupling spin and valley degrees of freedom with ferroelectricity and Berry-curvature driven transport in the absence of net magnetization. However, achieving nonvolatile and selective control of these intertwined degrees of freedom remains a key challenge. Here, using first-principles calculations, we show that the direction of interlayer sliding serves as a symmetry selective control parameter in altermagnetic bilayer Fe2WS4. Diagonal sliding breaks inversion symmetry and produces two sliding ferroelectric states with opposite out-of-plane polarizations. Reversal of the ferroelectric polarization switches the momentum-dependent spin texture and reverses the anomalous Hall conductivity, revealing strong magnetoelectric coupling and enabling a ferroelectrically switchable anomalous Hall effect. In contrast, axial sliding preserves inversion symmetry but breaks the crystalline symmetry relating the X and Y valleys, leading to reversible valley polarization and a switchable anomalous valley Hall effect. These results establish the direction of interlayer sliding as a nonvolatile symmetry selector for controlling ferroelectricity, spin texture, valley polarization, and Hall transport responses in two-dimensional altermagnetic bilayers.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that interlayer sliding direction in altermagnetic bilayer Fe2WS4 serves as a symmetry-selective control parameter for manipulating ferroelectricity, spin texture, valley polarization, and Hall transport. Diagonal sliding creates switchable ferroelectric states that control anomalous Hall effect, while axial sliding enables reversible valley polarization and anomalous valley Hall effect.
AI.motivation: The challenge lies in achieving nonvolatile and selective control of intertwined degrees of freedom (spin, valley, ferroelectricity) in altermagnets, which combine compensated magnetic order with momentum-dependent spin splitting but lack net magnetization. The authors seek a symmetry-based approach to control these properties.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were performed on altermagnetic bilayer Fe2WS4 to investigate how different directions of interlayer sliding affect symmetry and electronic properties.
AI.result: Diagonal sliding breaks inversion symmetry, producing two sliding ferroelectric states with opposite out-of-plane polarizations. Reversing ferroelectric polarization switches the momentum-dependent spin texture and reverses anomalous Hall conductivity, demonstrating strong magnetoelectric coupling. Axial sliding preserves inversion symmetry but breaks crystalline symmetry relating X and Y valleys, leading to reversible valley polarization and switchable anomalous valley Hall effect.
AI.conclusion: The direction of interlayer sliding acts as a nonvolatile symmetry selector for controlling ferroelectricity, spin texture, valley polarization, and Hall transport responses in 2D altermagnetic bilayers, establishing a new paradigm for multi-degree-of-freedom control in altermagnetic materials.

title: Spinless charged excitation at the interface between a conventional topological insulator and a topological Mott insulator
summary: We investigate the interface separating two topologically distinct insulating phases of matter using extensive density-matrix renormalization group calculations to study the triangular-lattice Hofstadter-Hubbard model with a spatially varying interaction strength, chosen to realize both integer quantum Hall and chiral spin liquid states in different spatial regions. We find that the integer quantum Hall-chiral spin liquid interface hosts a spinless charged excitation that is bound to the interface. This mode at the interface is identified through charge and spin pumping, and by direct calculations of low-lying excited states. We also characterize bulk excitations in both phases, finding evidence for fractionalization in the chiral spin liquid and for spin-triplet exciton formation in the integer quantum Hall phase.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the interface between integer quantum Hall and chiral spin liquid phases in the triangular-lattice Hofstadter-Hubbard model using DMRG calculations. The key finding is the discovery of a spinless charged excitation bound to the interface between these two topologically distinct phases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the nature of interfaces separating topologically distinct insulating phases of matter, specifically exploring what excitations emerge at such boundaries between integer quantum Hall and chiral spin liquid states.
AI.method: The study employs extensive density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) calculations on the triangular-lattice Hofstadter-Hubbard model with spatially varying interaction strength. The interface excitations are identified through charge and spin pumping measurements and direct calculations of low-lying excited states.
AI.result: A spinless charged excitation bound to the interface was discovered. The study also characterized bulk excitations in both phases, finding evidence for fractionalization in the chiral spin liquid and spin-triplet exciton formation in the integer quantum Hall phase.
AI.conclusion: The integer quantum Hall-chiral spin liquid interface hosts a novel spinless charged excitation bound to the interface, representing a key finding for understanding topological phase boundaries and interface physics in correlated electron systems.

title: Magnetic Order in bilayer Ruddlesden-Popper Nickelates
summary: The recent discovery of high-temperature superconductivity in the bilayer nickelate La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ has led to extensive interest in the correlation physics of its normal state. Given that the superconducitivity develops near a density wave order in the phase diagram, it is important to elucidate the nature of this order. Based on the accumulated experimental evidence for a bad metal state in proximity to an orbital-selective Mott phase, here we describe magnetic correlations of the system in a conceptually new way -- in terms of effective local moments experiencing a combination of RKKY and superexchange interactions. This gives rise to a magnetic order with a wavevector that is close to $\mathbf{Q}=(π/2,π/2)$ and, at the same time, yields a clear understanding of the associated spin dynamics. Our results are consistent with the rapidly emerging experiments about the magnetic correlations in the density wave order of the bilayer nickelate. Implications for unconventional superconductivity in this and related multiorbital systems are discussed.

title: Multistate ferroelectricity and switchable layer-locked anomalous valley Hall effects in bilayer ReIrGe2Se6
summary: Two-dimensional multiferroic materials, which combine magnetic and ferroelectric (FE) orders with strong magnetoelectric coupling, represent ideal platforms for high-density information storage and low-power multistate electronics. However, the intrinsic bistability of conventional ferroelectricity poses a substantial challenge to realizing multiple nonvolatile states and programmable Berry-curvature driven transport responses within a single material. Here, using first-principles calculations, we predict multistate ferroelectricity in AA0-stacked bilayer ReIrGe2Se6. The system hosts four energetically stable FE polarization configurations, among which three are connected through reversible switching pathways, while the fourth exhibits a unidirectional switching pathway. The distinct FE configurations further give rise to a cyclic semiconductor-metal-semiconductor evolution in the electronic structure. Notably, FE polarization switching is intimately coupled to layer degrees of freedom and Berry curvature. The layer-dependent electrostatic potential associated with different FE configurations controls the layer character of the band-edge states, thereby locking the Berry curvature to specific layer channels. As a result, bilayer ReIrGe2Se6 enables switching between an anomalous valley Hall effect and a layer-locked anomalous valley Hall effect, providing nonvolatile control of layer, valley, and spin-resolved transport responses. In addition, magnetization reversal switches the valley and spin channels while preserving the layer-resolved character. These results establish bilayer ReIrGe2Se6 as a multistate ferroelectric platform for programmable Berry-curvature related transport, offering microscopic insight into topology based multifunctional electronic and valleytronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper predicts multistate ferroelectricity in AA0-stacked bilayer ReIrGe2Se6 using first-principles calculations. The system exhibits four stable FE polarization configurations with different switching pathways, enabling programmable Berry-curvature driven transport responses including switching between normal and layer-locked anomalous valley Hall effects.
AI.motivation: Conventional ferroelectricity's intrinsic bistability limits the realization of multiple nonvolatile states and programmable Berry-curvature driven transport in a single material, which is needed for high-density information storage and low-power multistate electronics.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were used to predict multistate ferroelectricity in AA0-stacked bilayer ReIrGe2Se6, examining FE polarization configurations, switching pathways, electronic structure evolution, and Berry curvature coupling to layer degrees of freedom.
AI.result: Four energetically stable FE polarization configurations were found: three connected through reversible switching pathways and one with unidirectional switching. FE switching couples to layer degrees of freedom and Berry curvature, creating cyclic semiconductor-metal-semiconductor electronic evolution and enabling switching between anomalous valley Hall effect and layer-locked anomalous valley Hall effect.
AI.conclusion: Bilayer ReIrGe2Se6 serves as a multistate ferroelectric platform for programmable Berry-curvature related transport, offering microscopic insight for topology-based multifunctional electronic and valleytronic devices.

title: AutoHF: a general Hartree-Fock solver utilizing direct energy minimization with automatic differentiation
summary: We present autohf, a general, easy-to-use mean-field solver for quantum many-fermion Hamiltonians. It allows the user to bypass the process of deciphering the mean-field form for each many-body Hamiltonian $H$ and thus avoid setting up a tailored program for each $H$. Rather, autohf finds the optimal Slater determinant $|Ψ\rangle$, written in terms of orbital coefficients and subject to symmetry constraints, by directly minimizing the variational energy $\langle H \rangle$. By embracing this variational approach, autohf makes use of the growing power of automatic differentiation and optimization tools developed by the machine learning community.

AI.tldr: Autohf is a general, user-friendly mean-field solver for quantum many-fermion Hamiltonians that automatically finds optimal Slater determinants by directly minimizing variational energy, leveraging automatic differentiation and optimization tools from machine learning.
AI.motivation: The traditional approach to mean-field calculations requires users to manually decipher the mean-field form for each many-body Hamiltonian and develop tailored programs, which is cumbersome and inefficient. There was a need for a general-purpose tool that could handle various Hamiltonians without custom implementation for each case.
AI.method: The method involves directly minimizing the variational energy ⟨H⟩ to find the optimal Slater determinant |Ψ⟩, expressed in terms of orbital coefficients and subject to symmetry constraints. It utilizes automatic differentiation and optimization tools from the machine learning community.
AI.result: A general, easy-to-use mean-field solver (autohf) that can handle various quantum many-fermion Hamiltonians without requiring user-specific implementations or manual derivation of mean-field forms.
AI.conclusion: Autohf provides a versatile and accessible tool for mean-field calculations in quantum many-body systems, bridging computational physics with modern machine learning techniques to simplify and automate the variational optimization process.

title: High-Q superconducting microwave resonators using MBE titanium nitride
summary: Using molecular beam epitaxy, we have realized thin films of titanium nitride (TiN) on c-plane sapphire that exhibit the lowest observed full-width at half maximum X-ray rocking curve width of 18 arcsec. Though the (111) oriented TiN exhibits an abrupt and crystalline interface with sapphire, for the first time we observe sub-surface defects in the sapphire substrate, which nucleate structural defects in the epitaxial TiN layer. Using quarter-wavelength coplanar waveguide (CPW) resonators in a 3 \textmu m/6 \textmu m/3 \textmu m gap/strip/gap lines in a hanger geometry, we find the internal quality factor of the TiN resonators to be $>10^{6}$ in the single-photon $\langle n \rangle \sim 1$ limit at 5.8 GHz and 10 mK, rising to $>20 \times 10^{6}$ at $\langle n \rangle \sim 10^{6}$. The results are of high interest for applications of superconducting TiN in several areas, and provide a path towards epitaxial Josephson junctions with crystalline barriers in the future for high coherence qubits.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the growth of high-quality titanium nitride (TiN) thin films on c-plane sapphire substrates using molecular beam epitaxy, achieving record-low X-ray rocking curve widths of 18 arcsec and demonstrating superconducting resonators with exceptionally high quality factors (>10^6 at single-photon levels).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop superconducting TiN materials with high coherence for quantum computing applications. Previous attempts at epitaxial TiN growth likely suffered from defects and poor interface quality, limiting coherence times. This work aims to achieve epitaxial growth with minimal defects for quantum information processing.
AI.method: The researchers used molecular beam epitaxy to grow TiN thin films on c-plane sapphire substrates. They characterized the films using X-ray rocking curve measurements and microscopy. For device fabrication, they built quarter-wavelength coplanar waveguide resonators with 3 μm/6 μm/3 μm gap/strip/gap dimensions in a hanger geometry, then measured internal quality factors at 5.8 GHz and 10 mK across different photon number regimes.
AI.result: The key results include: (1) the lowest reported full-width at half maximum X-ray rocking curve width of 18 arcsec for TiN; (2) discovery of sub-surface defects in the sapphire substrate that nucleate structural defects in the TiN layer; (3) internal quality factors exceeding 10^6 at the single-photon limit (⟨n⟩ ~ 1), rising to >20 × 10^6 at higher photon numbers (⟨n⟩ ~ 10^6).
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that epitaxial TiN on sapphire can achieve exceptionally high coherence for superconducting qubits. The findings provide a pathway toward epitaxial Josephson junctions with crystalline barriers for future high-coherence quantum processors.

title: Fractional quantum ferroelectric control of spin-valley locking and valley Hall effects in altermagnetic monolayer Cr2S2
summary: Fractional quantum multiferroics, arising from the coupling between fractional quantum ferroelectricity (FQFE) and altermagnetism (AM), provide a promising platform for nonvolatile control of momentum dependent spin splitting in systems with zero net magnetization. However, extending this FQFE-AM coupling to valley degrees of freedom and Berry curvature driven valley Hall effects remains largely unexplored. Here, using first-principles calculations, we demonstrate that monolayer Cr2S2 realizes a two dimensional FQFE-AM platform with two switchable FQFE states connected by composite symmetry operations combining a fractional lattice translation with time reversal or parity-time reversal. We show that FQFE switching reverses the AM spin-polarized band structure and interchanges the spin characters of the X and Y valleys without rotating the Néel vector, thereby enabling polarization switchable spin-valley locking. Moreover, the two FQFE states exhibit reversed Berry curvature distributions, which, together with the switched spin-valley locking, enable polarization controlled valley Hall effects under both electron and hole doping. These results demonstrate a symmetry based mechanism for nonvolatile electrical control of AM spin splitting, spin-valley locking, and valley Hall effects, offering a general route toward low-power valleytronic devices based on FQFE-AM coupling.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that monolayer Cr2S2 is a 2D fractional quantum ferroelectric (FQFE)-altermagnetic (AM) platform where FQFE switching can nonvoltably control spin-valley locking and valley Hall effects through symmetry operations, offering a pathway for low-power valleytronic devices.
AI.motivation: Previous work on FQFE-AM coupling focused on momentum-dependent spin splitting but hadn't been extended to valley degrees of freedom and Berry curvature-driven valley Hall effects. The researchers aimed to fill this gap by exploring valley physics in FQFE-AM systems.
AI.method: The researchers used first-principles calculations to investigate monolayer Cr2S2 as a candidate material for observing these phenomena.
AI.result: They found that monolayer Cr2S2 exhibits two switchable FQFE states connected by composite symmetry operations (fractional lattice translation combined with time reversal or parity-time reversal). FQFE switching reverses the AM spin-polarized band structure, interchanges spin characters of X and Y valleys without rotating the Néel vector, enables polarization switchable spin-valley locking, and creates reversed Berry curvature distributions in the two FQFE states. These features allow polarization-controlled valley Hall effects under both electron and hole doping.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a symmetry-based mechanism for nonvolatile electrical control of AM spin splitting, spin-valley locking, and valley Hall effects, providing a general route toward low-power valleytronic devices based on FQFE-AM coupling.

title: The two-particle density matrix of a Luttinger liquid
comment: 24 pages, 14 figures. For associated data and code repository see: https://github.com/DelMaestroGroup/papers-code-TwoRDMLuttingerLiquid
summary: Two-particle coherence is the first level of the reduced-density-matrix hierarchy that contains correlations inaccessible to single-particle observables, yet analytic two-particle density matrices are rare even in one dimension. We derive a closed, finite-size expression for the equal-time two-particle reduced density matrix of spinless fermions in a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid using constructive bosonization with an explicit ultraviolet cutoff. In addition to the familiar Luttinger parameter $K$-dependent exponent $γ^2=(K+K^{-1}-2)/2$ which governs the spatial decay of matrix elements, the result exposes a second exponent, $λ=(K^{-1}-K)/2$, which encodes correlations between opposite chiralities and controls the off-diagonal structure. The diagonal limit of the two-particle reduced density matrix yields density correlations and the static structure factor, while its coherences resolve algebraic $2k_F$ charge-density-wave correlations for repulsion and odd-parity p-wave pairing correlations for attraction. After fixing the cutoff from the one-particle density matrix, the analytic result quantitatively reproduces density matrix renormalization group calculations of the interacting J-V chain within the Luttinger liquid regime. The result connects universal Luttinger liquid scaling with observables in finite microscopic systems.

AI.tldr: The paper derives an exact closed-form expression for the equal-time two-particle reduced density matrix (2RDM) of spinless fermions in a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid using constructive bosonization with an explicit UV cutoff. This is significant because analytic 2RDMs are extremely rare even in 1D systems, yet the 2RDM is the lowest level of the reduced density matrix hierarchy containing correlations beyond single-particle observables.
AI.motivation: Two-particle coherence represents the first level of the reduced-density-matrix hierarchy that contains correlations inaccessible to single-particle observables. However, analytic two-particle density matrices are rare even in one dimension, limiting understanding of correlation effects in Luttinger liquids.
AI.method: The authors use constructive bosonization with an explicit ultraviolet cutoff to derive a closed, finite-size expression for the equal-time two-particle reduced density matrix of spinless fermions in a Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid.
AI.result: The result reveals two distinct exponents: (1) γ² = (K+K⁻¹-2)/2, which governs the spatial decay of matrix elements and was previously known; (2) λ = (K⁻¹-K)/2, a newly identified exponent encoding correlations between opposite chiralities that controls the off-diagonal structure. The diagonal yields density correlations and the static structure factor. The coherences resolve algebraic 2k_F charge-density-wave correlations for repulsion and odd-parity p-wave pairing correlations for attraction. After fixing the cutoff from the one-particle density matrix, the analytic result quantitatively reproduces DMRG calculations of the interacting J-V chain within the Luttinger liquid regime.
AI.conclusion: The result establishes a connection between universal Luttinger liquid scaling and observables in finite microscopic systems, providing a quantitative bridge between theory and numerical calculations.

title: Altermagnetic spin textures coupled to superconductors: Domain wall spin-triplet superconductivity and supercurrent-induced torques
summary: Motivated by the absence of sizable stray fields and the recently discovered highly non-trivial impact of altermagnetic textures on itinerant electrons, we here study the form of Cooper pairs in spatially varying altermagnets coupled to conventional $s$-wave superconductors. As a consequence of the detrimental impact of altermagnetism on spin-singlet pairing and the local symmetry reduction caused by textures in the magnetic order parameter, we show that superconductivity predominantly impacts the regions between altermagnetic domains. Focusing on a planar radial domain wall for concreteness, we show that emergent Zeeman and spin-orbit fields create spatially separated triplet hotspots and transitions between nodal and fully gapped superconducting regions, whose structure is set by both the domain wall and the altermagnetic order parameter. We also identify a reciprocal effect, where a supercurrent generates a quasiparticle-mediated quadrupolar torque that inherits the symmetry of the altermagnetic order. Our results show that accounting for spatial inhomogeneities in the altermagnetic order parameter is essential for an understanding of the superconducting proximity effect and suggest that hybrid systems of altermagnetic textures and superconductors offer unique opportunities for local engineering of Cooper pairs and for detecting altermagnetic order.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Cooper pair formation in spatially varying altermagnets coupled to s-wave superconductors, discovering that superconductivity localizes between altermagnetic domains with emergent triplet hotspots and gapped regions controlled by domain wall geometry.
AI.motivation: The absence of sizable stray fields in altermagnets and the recently discovered highly non-trivial impact of altermagnetic textures on itinerant electrons motivated this study, as prior research lacked understanding of how spatial variations in altermagnetic order affect superconducting proximity effects.
AI.method: The authors analyze a planar radial domain wall configuration to examine how altermagnetic textures couple to conventional s-wave superconductors, investigating emergent Zeeman and spin-orbit fields, and exploring reciprocal effects where supercurrents generate quasiparticle-mediated quadrupolar torque.
AI.result: The study reveals that superconductivity predominantly impacts regions between altermagnetic domains; emergent Zeeman and spin-orbit fields create spatially separated triplet hotspots and transitions between nodal and fully gapped superconducting regions; a reciprocal effect was identified where supercurrent generates a quadrupolar torque inheriting altermagnetic symmetry.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for spatial inhomogeneities in altermagnetic order is essential for understanding superconducting proximity effects; hybrid altermagnet-superconductor systems offer unique opportunities for local engineering of Cooper pairs and detecting altermagnetic order.

title: Second-Order Optical Nonlinearity of AlScN Films Grown By Molecular Beam Epitaxy
summary: Alloys of AlN have rapidly emerged as a material platform for nonlinear optics. In this paper, we measure the second-order optical nonlinearity of AlScN films grown directly on nitrided c-plane sapphire by molecular beam epitaxy. This direct growth approach, which bypasses a thick AlN buffer layer, allows us to isolate the true nonlinear response of the AlScN film. Our results show a large enhancement of d31, but a suppression of d33 in AlScN films compared to AlN. We observe that d31 can be as high as 4.92 pm/V , which is 60 times larger than that of AlN. The development of AlScN-based photonic devices can enable energy-efficient nonlinear optical operations that can be epitaxially integrated with electronic and photonic devices based on Si, GaN and AlN.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the measurement of second-order optical nonlinearity in AlScN films grown directly on nitrided c-plane sapphire by molecular beam epitaxy. The study发现 d31 coefficient up to 4.92 pm/V, which is 60 times larger than AlN, while d33 is suppressed compared to AlN. This direct growth approach bypasses thick AlN buffer layers to isolate the true nonlinear response of AlScN.
AI.motivation: Alloys of AlN have emerged as a promising platform for nonlinear optics, but the true nonlinear response of AlScN films needed to be isolated and measured. Previous studies used thick AlN buffer layers which may have obscured the intrinsic properties of AlScN.
AI.method: AlScN films were grown directly on nitrided c-plane sapphire using molecular beam epitaxy, bypassing the traditional thick AlN buffer layer approach. The second-order optical nonlinearity (d31 and d33 coefficients) was measured to characterize the nonlinear response.
AI.result: The study found a large enhancement of the d31 coefficient (up to 4.92 pm/V), which is 60 times larger than that of pure AlN. However, the d33 coefficient was found to be suppressed in AlScN compared to AlN.
AI.conclusion: AlScN shows significant potential for energy-efficient nonlinear optical operations, with the enhanced d31 coefficient making it particularly valuable. The material can be epitaxially integrated with electronic and photonic devices based on Si, GaN, and AlN, enabling new integrated photonic systems.

title: Emergence of a monopole phase in the $J_1{-}J_2$ Heisenberg model on the triangular lattice for small magnetic fields
summary: We investigate the ground-state phase diagram of the $J_1{-}J_2$ Heisenberg model on the triangular lattice under an external Zeeman field $H$ by using the variational Monte Carlo approach. We span a region with $0 \le J_2/J_1 \le 0.2$ and $0 \le H/J_1 \le 2$, to assess the fate of the (putative) spin-liquid phase that has been detected for $J_2/J_1=1/8$ at zero magnetic field. Simple variational ansatze are proposed for a few candidate states, and their energetics are compared on large clusters to obtain the phase diagram. For $J_2/J_1 \lesssim 1/6$, a continuous transition from a gapless "Y'' phase to a gapped "up-up-down'' phase is obtained, as predicted by spin-wave theory. Most importantly, around $J_2/J_1=1/8$, a condensate of monopoles (which are gapless gauge excitations of the spin liquid at $H=0$) is stabilized in a significant region of the phase diagram, for small Zeeman fields. Here, a finite scalar chirality is present, while no transverse magnetic order is detected. The stability of the monopole phase is confirmed by a field-theory approach that includes a self-consistent random-phase approximation of the low-lying spin fluctuations. The boundary between the monopole and "Y'' phases is also obtained with no free parameters.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the ground-state phase diagram of the J1-J2 Heisenberg model on a triangular lattice under external Zeeman field using variational Monte Carlo. It explores whether the spin-liquid phase at J2/J1=1/8 persists in magnetic fields and identifies a monopole condensate phase stabilized in a region of the phase diagram.
AI.motivation: To assess the fate of the putative spin-liquid phase detected at J2/J1=1/8 in zero magnetic field when an external Zeeman field is applied, and to map out the complete phase diagram in the parameter region 0 ≤ J2/J1 ≤ 0.2 and 0 ≤ H/J1 ≤ 2.
AI.method: Variational Monte Carlo approach with simple variational ansatze for candidate states, comparing energetics on large clusters. Field-theory approach with self-consistent random-phase approximation for low-lying spin fluctuations.
AI.result: For J2/J1 ≲ 1/6, a continuous transition from gapless "Y'' phase to gapped "up-up-down'' phase is found. Around J2/J1=1/8, a monopole condensate phase is stabilized for small Zeeman fields, exhibiting finite scalar chirality without transverse magnetic order. The monopole phase boundary is obtained with no free parameters.
AI.conclusion: The spin-liquid phase at J2/J1=1/8 evolves into a stable monopole condensate phase under small Zeeman fields, characterized by finite scalar chirality and no transverse magnetic order, existing in a significant region of the phase diagram.

title: Pressure-Driven Structural Transitions without a Displacive Charge-Density Wave in La$_2$SmNi$_2$O$_7$
summary: We investigated the structural properties of bilayer nickelate La$_2$SmNi$_2$O$_7$ as a function of pressure and temperature. At ambient conditions, we show that the material crystallizes as a monoclinic superstructure distinct from the one previously reported and close to the pseudo-orthorhombic structure of pristine La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$. No signatures of satellite reflections associated with charge density wave (CDW) ordering are detected at low temperature. Upon compression, a sequence of pressure-induced structural transitions from monoclinic to orthorhombic 15 GPa and then tetragonal 21 GPa symmetry is observed. Within the superconducting dome, the quality of the X-ray diffraction data enables structural refinements enabling theoretical models to understand the emergence of superconductivity.

AI.tldr: This study investigates structural properties of bilayer nickelate La₂SmNi₂O₇ under pressure and temperature variations, revealing a previously unreported monoclinic superstructure at ambient conditions and pressure-induced structural transitions (monoclinic→orthorhombic→tetragonal) within the superconducting dome.
AI.motivation: Understand the structural evolution of bilayer nickelates and their relationship to superconductivity, as these materials are potential analogues to cuprate superconductors.
AI.method: X-ray diffraction measurements under varying pressure and temperature conditions, with structural refinements.
AI.result: Material crystallizes in a monoclinic superstructure distinct from previous reports; no CDW signatures detected at low temperature; sequential structural transitions occur at 15 GPa (orthorhombic) and 21 GPa (tetragonal).
AI.conclusion: The structural transitions within the superconducting dome provide insights for theoretical models to understand superconductivity emergence in bilayer nickelates.

title: Direct observation of anisotropic exciton dispersion in the 2D semiconductor CrSBr
summary: We report momentum-resolved measurements of exciton dispersion in multilayer CrSBr using defocus-engineered electron energy-loss spectroscopy, supported by first-principles calculations. A pronounced in-plane anisotropy is observed, with the exciton exhibiting a linear dispersion along $Γ$Y within $\lvert \boldsymbol{q} \rvert$ < 0.007 Å$^{-1}$, while remaining nearly dispersionless along $Γ$X. The slope reaches 7.02 eV Å, among the largest reported in low-dimensional systems. The calculations reproduce the experimentally observed linear dispersion, confirming its intrinsic origin. We attribute the anisotropic dispersion to the long-range electron--hole exchange interaction, enhanced by strong out-of-plane confinement and governed by the directional selection rules of the transition dipole moment. Comparative measurements across the magnetic phase transition from the paramagnetic to the A-type antiferromagnetic state show that the dispersion remains essentially unchanged, indicating negligible coupling between exciton propagation and magnetic order. These results establish CrSBr as a model system for investigating anisotropic exciton dynamics in low-symmetry layered semiconductors.

AI.tldr: This study investigates exciton dispersion in multilayer CrSBr using defocus-engineered electron energy-loss spectroscopy and first-principles calculations. A pronounced in-plane anisotropy was found, with the exciton showing linear dispersion along ΓY direction (slope: 7.02 eV Å) but remaining nearly dispersionless along ΓX. This anisotropic behavior is attributed to long-range electron-hole exchange interactions enhanced by out-of-plane confinement and directional selection rules. The dispersion remains unchanged across the magnetic phase transition, indicating negligible coupling between exciton propagation and magnetic order.
AI.motivation: Investigate anisotropic exciton dynamics in low-symmetry layered semiconductors and understand the origin of exciton dispersion in CrSBr
AI.method: Defocus-engineered electron energy-loss spectroscopy for momentum-resolved measurements, supported by first-principles calculations
AI.result: Pronounced in-plane anisotropy observed: linear dispersion along ΓY within |q| < 0.007 Å⁻¹ with slope of 7.02 eV Å (among largest in low-dimensional systems), nearly dispersionless along ΓX. Calculations reproduce the linear dispersion. Anisotropic dispersion attributed to long-range electron-hole exchange interaction enhanced by strong out-of-plane confinement and governed by directional selection rules of transition dipole moment. Dispersion remains essentially unchanged across magnetic phase transition from paramagnetic to A-type antiferromagnetic state.
AI.conclusion: CrSBr is established as a model system for investigating anisotropic exciton dynamics in low-symmetry layered semiconductors, with negligible coupling between exciton propagation and magnetic order.

title: Monopole Spin Density Wave States in Magnetic Weyl Semimetals
summary: The interplay between topology and magnetism in Weyl semimetals has recently emerged as a fertile ground for novel quantum phases. While monopole harmonic order parameters have been established for superconductivity and charge density waves in these systems, their spin density wave counterparts remain unexplored. Here we introduce monopole spin density wave (SDW) states arising from particle-hole pairing between nested Fermi surfaces enclosing Weyl nodes of the same chirality. We demonstrate that the SDW order parameter inherits a nontrivial pairing Berry phase and is described by monopole harmonic functions that exhibit topologically protected nodal structures in the gap function. Through a concrete lattice model, we show that helical and cycloidal SDW orders produce distinct signatures in band structures, Fermi arc distributions, and surface spin polarization patterns, which can be directly resolved by spin- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy. Remarkably, we find that the quantum geometric tensor of the monopole pairing realizes ideal quantum geometry in the weak-coupling limit, where quantum distance fluctuations are entirely governed by Berry curvature. Our results not only unify the understanding of monopole ordered states across pairing channels but also provide experimental avenues for distinguishing competing magnetic orders in ReAlX (Re=rare earth elements, X=Si, Ge) materials and suggest potential applications in topological spintronics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces monopole spin density wave (SDW) states in Weyl semimetals, arising from particle-hole pairing between nested Fermi surfaces enclosing Weyl nodes of same chirality. The SDW order parameter inherits a nontrivial pairing Berry phase and exhibits topologically protected nodal structures. Different SDW orders (helical and cycloidal) produce distinct signatures detectable by spin-resolved photoemission spectroscopy.
AI.motivation: While monopole harmonic order parameters have been established for superconductivity and charge density waves in Weyl semimetals, their spin density wave counterparts remained unexplored. This work aims to fill this gap and unify understanding of monopole ordered states across different pairing channels.
AI.method: The authors developed a theoretical framework using monopole harmonic functions to describe SDW order parameters, and used a concrete lattice model to demonstrate the properties. They analyzed quantum geometric tensor to characterize the quantum geometry of monopole pairing.
AI.result: Helical and cycloidal SDW orders produce distinct signatures in band structures, Fermi arc distributions, and surface spin polarization patterns. The quantum geometric tensor realizes ideal quantum geometry in the weak-coupling limit, where quantum distance fluctuations are entirely governed by Berry curvature.
AI.conclusion: This work unifies understanding of monopole ordered states across pairing channels and provides experimental avenues for distinguishing competing magnetic orders in ReAlX materials, with potential applications in topological spintronics.

title: Recent progress on liquid transport growth of quantum materials
summary: Liquid transport growth (LTG) is a horizontal flux growth technique that is closely analogous to chemical vapor transport, with the key distinction that a molten flux rather than a vapor serves as the transport agent. Unlike conventional flux growth, LTG spatially separates charge dissolution and crystal precipitation and couples them through continuous solute transport under a deliberately imposed temperature gradient. This enables crystal growth to begin before the charge is completely dissolved, removes the equilibrium solubility constraint on the starting charge/flux ratio, and allows large yields of single crystals to be obtained from a single growth. Recent studies have further shown that by spatially separating dissolution and crystallization and maintaining crystallization at a nearly constant temperature, LTG is particularly effective for two classes of materials: compounds that crystallize only within a narrow temperature and/or composition window, and compounds whose stoichiometry, defect concentration, and thus physical properties are sensitive to the crystallization temperature. In this review, we discuss representative examples including Fe$_3$Sn$_2$, CrTe$_3$, YFe$_2$Ge$_2$, UTe$_2$, CeRh$_2$As$_2$, MoTe$_2$, WTe$_2$, and LuNb$_6$Sn$_6$ to illustrate the unique capabilities of LTG for producing high quality single crystals of diverse quantum materials. We also summarize practical considerations for LTG experimental design, including furnace selection, growth time, melt stability, and ampoule geometry, and discuss future opportunities for transforming LTG from an empirical growth method into a more predictive crystal growth technique.

AI.tldr: Liquid Transport Growth (LTG) is a horizontal flux growth technique that uses molten flux rather than vapor as transport agent, enabling spatial separation of charge dissolution and crystal precipitation with continuous solute transport under temperature gradient. This review discusses LTG's application to quantum materials including Fe3Sn2, CrTe3, YFe2Ge2, UTe2, CeRh2As2, MoTe2, WTe2, and LuNb6Sn6, along with practical experimental design considerations.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of conventional flux growth methods, particularly for materials that crystallize within narrow temperature/composition windows or have stoichiometry sensitive to crystallization temperature. LTG enables crystal growth before complete charge dissolution and removes equilibrium solubility constraints on starting charge/flux ratios.
AI.method: Horizontal flux growth technique using molten flux as transport agent under deliberately imposed temperature gradient. Spatially separates dissolution and crystallization zones while coupling them through continuous solute transport. Maintains crystallization at nearly constant temperature.
AI.result: Successfully demonstrated growth of high-quality single crystals from diverse quantum materials including Fe3Sn2, CrTe3, YFe2Ge2, UTe2, CeRh2As2, MoTe2, WTe2, and LuNb6Sn6. Enables large yields from single growths and effective for compounds with narrow crystallization windows.
AI.conclusion: LTG represents a powerful technique for growing single crystals of quantum materials, particularly those with challenging crystallization requirements. Future work should focus on transforming LTG from empirical to predictive approach through improved understanding of transport mechanisms and optimization of experimental parameters.

title: Tunable Magneto-Excitonic Coupling in Alloyed van der Waals Antiferromagnet
summary: The unique coupling between magnetic order and photo-generated excitons, electron-hole pairs bound by Coulomb interaction, in layered magnetic semiconductors offers a powerful mechanism for controlling light-matter interactions. In the van der Waals antiferromagnet CrSBr, this coupling is exceptionally strong and manifests distinctly between two coexisting excitonic states: the localised, Frenkel-like XA exciton and the more delocalised, Wannier-Mott-like XB exciton, providing a unique playground for the optical control of magnetism. Here, we reveal how chlorine incorporation reshapes the magneto-optical interplay in CrSBr1-xClx by simultaneously modifying its electronic structure, excitonic properties, and magnetic interactions. Combining magneto-optical spectroscopy up to 85 T with state-of-the-art quasiparticle self-consistent GW (QSGW) calculations on alloy supercells, we show that Cl insertion progressively localises the excitonic wavefunctions and drives both states toward a more Frenkel-like regime. This evolution is accompanied by a systematic reduction of the magnetic-field-induced energy renormalisation, most prominently for the XB exciton. Our work connects exciton character directly to magneto-excitonic coupling. Furthermore, it establishes compositional alloying as an effective strategy for engineering the coupling between magnetic and optical properties in van der Waals magnetic semiconductors.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how chlorine incorporation in the van der Waals antiferromagnet CrSBr affects the coupling between magnetic order and photo-generated excitons, revealing that Cl insertion localizes excitonic wavefunctions and reduces magnetic-field-induced energy renormalization.
AI.motivation: The unique coupling between magnetic order and excitons in layered magnetic semiconductors offers a mechanism for controlling light-matter interactions. In CrSBr, this coupling is exceptionally strong but needs to be engineered for practical applications.
AI.method: The researchers combined magneto-optical spectroscopy up to 85 T with quasiparticle self-consistent GW (QSGW) calculations on alloy supercells to study CrSBr1-xClx.
AI.result: Chlorine incorporation progressively localizes excitonic wavefunctions, driving both XA and XB exciton states toward a more Frenkel-like regime. This evolution is accompanied by a systematic reduction of magnetic-field-induced energy renormalization, most prominently for the XB exciton.
AI.conclusion: Compositional alloying is an effective strategy for engineering the coupling between magnetic and optical properties in van der Waals magnetic semiconductors. The work connects exciton character directly to magneto-excitonic coupling.

title: Optimization dynamics of Transformer backflow neural quantum states for the two-dimensional Hubbard model
summary: Building on the multi-determinant Transformer backflow neural quantum state (NQS) ansatz and the associated multi-stage training workflow for the doped two-dimensional Hubbard model, we investigate how the optimization dynamics of the NQS depend on several key optimization and architectural hyperparameters. The workflow consists of neural-network backflow (NNB) initialization, supervised Transformer pre-training, and main energy optimization using the Moment-Adaptive ReConfiguration Heuristic (MARCH) within variational Monte Carlo. Using the doped $4\times4$ periodic Hubbard model at $U=8$ as a baseline, we examine how the update-norm threshold, Transformer width, number of determinant channels, and Monte Carlo batch size affect convergence. We find that a moderate update constraint improves the efficiency of MARCH optimization, larger Transformer width and more determinant channels improve the expressive capacity of the ansatz, and larger Monte Carlo batches reduce sampling noise in the update direction. We further test the same workflow at half filling, weaker interaction strength, open boundary conditions, and on a larger $8\times8$ doped lattice. These results identify practical optimization trends for Transformer backflow NQSs and highlight the balance between ansatz expressivity, MARCH update stability, and Monte Carlo sampling quality.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates optimization dynamics of multi-determinant Transformer backflow neural quantum state (NQS) ansatz for the doped 2D Hubbard model, examining key hyperparameters like update-norm threshold, Transformer width, determinant channels, and Monte Carlo batch size.
AI.motivation: Understand how various optimization and architectural hyperparameters affect convergence of NQS for quantum many-body systems, particularly the doped Hubbard model.
AI.method: Multi-stage training workflow: (1) Neural-network backflow (NNB) initialization, (2) supervised Transformer pre-training, (3) main energy optimization using MARCH (Moment-Adaptive ReConfiguration Heuristic) within variational Monte Carlo. Tests performed on 4×4 periodic Hubbard model at U=8, with additional tests at half filling, weaker interaction, open boundary conditions, and 8×8 lattice.
AI.result: Moderate update constraint improves MARCH efficiency; larger Transformer width and more determinant channels enhance expressivity; larger Monte Carlo batches reduce sampling noise. These trends hold across different filling, interaction strengths, boundary conditions, and system sizes.
AI.conclusion: Practical optimization guidelines for Transformer backflow NQSs: balance between ansatz expressivity, MARCH update stability, and Monte Carlo sampling quality is crucial for successful convergence in quantum many-body simulations.

title: Driven-dissipative superconductivity in moiré heterostructure without attraction
summary: Dissipative preparation of quantum order offers a route to superconductivity that does not rely on enhancing attractive interactions. Here we propose a driven-dissipative protocol to prepare superconductivity as a stationary state of a two-dimensional moiré heterostructure. The key ingredient is a bilayer moiré platform in which the layer degree of freedom acts as a pseudospin, allowing the pseudospin structure required for pairing to be implemented through optically induced spatial operations. This preparation scheme requires local dissipation, which we show to arises naturally from weakly dispersive bosonic modes in the heterostructure. In contrast, in the opposite regime of collective dissipation, the same platform exhibits an early-time superradiant burst. Our results establish driven-dissipative moiré heterostructures as a promising platform for preparing superconductivity, while also revealing a connection between steady-state pairing and transient superradiance.

AI.tldr: A driven-dissipative protocol is proposed to prepare superconductivity as a stationary state in 2D bilayer moiré heterostructures, using the layer degree of freedom as pseudospin, with local dissipation arising from weakly dispersive bosonic modes, while collective dissipation leads to superradiant bursts.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to superconductivity rely on enhancing attractive interactions. This work seeks an alternative route through dissipative quantum state preparation that doesn't depend on such interactions.
AI.method: Driven-dissipative protocol using bilayer moiré platform where pseudospin structure for pairing is implemented through optically induced spatial operations. Local dissipation naturally arises from weakly dispersive bosonic modes in the heterostructure.
AI.result: The protocol successfully prepares superconductivity as a stationary state. In the collective dissipation regime, an early-time superradiant burst is observed instead.
AI.conclusion: Driven-dissipative moiré heterostructures are promising platforms for preparing superconductivity, revealing an unexpected connection between steady-state pairing and transient superradiance.

title: Boson peak and medium-range elastic heterogeneity in calcium silicate hydrate probed by terahertz spectroscopy and low-temperature calorimetry
summary: The boson peak (BP), a universal vibrational anomaly of disordered solids, has been predicted but not systematically characterized in calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H), the binding phase of hardened cement. Building on a preliminary terahertz survey, we characterize the BP across five Ca/Si ratios (0.5-1.7) using terahertz time-domain spectroscopy (THz-TDS) and low-temperature calorimetry, two probes of vibrational dynamics that complement the static picture of conventional structural methods. After Bruggeman correction for crystalline impurities, both probes locate the BP near 1 THz; they agree on frequency but diverge in intensity. The terahertz integrated spectral weight and the calorimetric Cp/T3 peak both fall monotonically with Ca/Si, whereas the apparent terahertz peak height is maximal at Ca/Si = 1.0, where damping is low and oscillator strength still substantial. This decoupling marks a structural crossover between silicate-chain depolymerization and interlayer calcium filling. From the BP we obtain a medium-range dynamical correlation length of order 1 nm (0.3-2 nm) and a coherent-potential elastic-heterogeneity parameter that decreases from gamma = 0.98 to 0.48 as Ca/Si rises; the Debye-normalized BP frequency (nu_BP/nu_D = 0.15-0.17) places C-S-H within the range reported for silicate glasses. Because gamma governs the distribution of energy barriers for local structural rearrangements, it provides a quantitative, composition-resolved descriptor relevant to the intrinsic creep and thermal transport of C-S-H, linking nanoscale vibrational dynamics to the macroscopic durability of concrete. The dual-probe boson-peak approach is transferable to other amorphous solids, including the supplementary cementitious materials of low-carbon cements.

AI.tldr: This study characterizes the boson peak (BP) vibrational anomaly in calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) across five Ca/Si ratios using terahertz time-domain spectroscopy and low-temperature calorimetry, revealing a composition-dependent dynamical correlation length of ~1 nm and linking nanoscale vibrational dynamics to macroscopic concrete durability.
AI.motivation: The boson peak, a universal vibrational anomaly in disordered solids, had been predicted but not systematically characterized in C-S-H, the binding phase of hardened cement. Understanding this feature is crucial for linking nanoscale vibrational dynamics to macroscopic properties like creep and thermal transport in concrete.
AI.method: The study used two complementary probes: terahertz time-domain spectroscopy (THz-TDS) and low-temperature calorimetry, across five Ca/Si ratios (0.5-1.7). Bruggerman correction was applied for crystalline impurities.
AI.result: Both probes locate the BP near 1 THz with agreement on frequency but divergence in intensity. The BP yields a medium-range dynamical correlation length of 1 nm (0.3-2 nm) and a coherent-potential elastic-heterogeneity parameter decreasing from gamma = 0.98 to 0.48 as Ca/Si rises. The Debye-normalized BP frequency (nu_BP/nu_D = 0.15-0.17) places C-S-H within the range of silicate glasses.
AI.conclusion: The decoupling between probes marks a structural crossover between silicate-chain depolymerization and interlayer calcium filling. The parameter gamma provides a quantitative, composition-resolved descriptor relevant to intrinsic creep and thermal transport, linking nanoscale vibrational dynamics to macroscopic concrete durability. The dual-probe approach is transferable to other amorphous solids including supplementary cementitious materials.

title: Sizable Ligand-Mediated Bond-Dependent Interactions in a Spin-1 Triangular Antiferromagnet NiI$_2$
summary: The bond-dependent anisotropic Kitaev interactions are the key for the Kitaev model, which has attracted intense interest for its potential to host quantum-spin-liquid states and fractional excitations. However, experimental realizations of such interactions remain scarce. Here, we investigate the magnetic excitations of NiI$_2$, a van der Waals magnet with spin $S=1$. By combining inelastic neutron scattering, magnetization measurements, magnetic structure analysis, first-principles calculations, and linear-spin-wave simulations, we identify a minimal model that features substantial Kitaev and off-diagonal $Γ$ interactions, which together stabilize the canted magnetic ground state and open a gap in the spin-wave spectrum. Notably, these interactions arise from strong spin-orbit coupling on the ligand ions, despite the quenched orbital moment of the magnetic Ni$^{2+}$ ions. Our results provide compelling experimental evidence for the ligand-driven Kitaev mechanism. This demonstrates a concrete pathway to generating strong bond-dependent anisotropy in systems where the magnetic ions themselves have weak spin-orbit coupling, thereby substantially broadening the range of potential Kitaev materials.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates NiI2, a van der Waals magnet with S=1, to identify the mechanism behind bond-dependent anisotropic Kitaev interactions. Using inelastic neutron scattering, magnetization measurements, magnetic structure analysis, first-principles calculations, and linear-spin-wave simulations, the researchers identify a minimal model with substantial Kitaev and off-diagonal Γ interactions that stabilize a canted magnetic ground state and open a gap in the spin-wave spectrum. The key finding is that these interactions arise from strong spin-orbit coupling on the ligand ions, despite the quenched orbital moment of magnetic Ni2+ ions, providing evidence for a ligand-driven Kitaev mechanism.
AI.motivation: Experimental realizations of bond-dependent anisotropic Kitaev interactions remain scarce despite their importance for quantum-spin-liquid states and fractional excitations. The researchers sought to investigate NiI2 as a potential Kitaev material and understand the origin of such interactions.
AI.method: The study combined multiple experimental and computational techniques: inelastic neutron scattering, magnetization measurements, magnetic structure analysis, first-principles calculations, and linear-spin-wave simulations to investigate the magnetic excitations of NiI2.
AI.result: The researchers identified a minimal model featuring substantial Kitaev and off-diagonal Γ interactions that stabilize a canted magnetic ground state and open a gap in the spin-wave spectrum. Notably, these interactions arise from strong spin-orbit coupling on the ligand ions, despite the quenched orbital moment of the magnetic Ni2+ ions.
AI.conclusion: This work provides compelling experimental evidence for the ligand-driven Kitaev mechanism, demonstrating a concrete pathway to generating strong bond-dependent anisotropy in systems where magnetic ions have weak spin-orbit coupling, thereby broadening the range of potential Kitaev materials.

title: Computing binary alloy phase diagrams with explicit configurational and vibrational entropy
summary: Phase stability in multicomponent solid solutions depends on configurational entropy beyond the ideal mixing limit, but capturing it together with vibrational entropy within the same atomistic framework remains challenging. Here, we extend non-equilibrium thermodynamic integration to composition-dependent transformations through an alchemical interpolation of the interactions, combined with Monte Carlo identity exchange moves and molecular dynamics that sample the vibrational and non-ideal configurational entropy along the integration path. We apply the framework to the Au-Cu binary alloy using Atomic Cluster Expansion potentials trained on density functional theory data using the LDA, PBE, and r2SCAN functionals, and construct composition-temperature phase diagrams directly from atomistic free energies. We find that explicit configurational sampling lowers the AuCu order-disorder transition temperature predicted by the ACE potential trained on LDA data from approximately 810 K to 710 K, closer to the experimental value of 683 K, and substantially widens the stability range of the solid solution. At the same time, the much larger sensitivity to the exchange-correlation functional shows that this level of agreement should not be interpreted as general predictive accuracy. Non-ideal configurational entropy must therefore be sampled explicitly, alongside a careful choice of functional, for a reliable atomistic description of binary phase diagrams.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of capturing both configurational and vibrational entropy in predicting phase stability of multicomponent solid solutions. The authors extend non-equilibrium thermodynamic integration to composition-dependent transformations using alchemical interpolation combined with Monte Carlo identity exchange and molecular dynamics. Applied to Au-Cu binary alloy, they find that explicit configurational sampling lowers the order-disorder transition temperature from ~810 K to 710 K (closer to experimental 683 K) and widens solid solution stability, while also revealing significant sensitivity to exchange-correlation functionals.
AI.motivation: Phase stability in multicomponent solid solutions depends on configurational entropy beyond the ideal mixing limit, but capturing it together with vibrational entropy within the same atomistic framework remains challenging. The authors aimed to develop a method to reliably predict binary phase diagrams from atomistic free energies.
AI.method: The authors extended non-equilibrium thermodynamic integration to composition-dependent transformations through: (1) alchemical interpolation of interactions, (2) Monte Carlo identity exchange moves, and (3) molecular dynamics sampling of vibrational and non-ideal configurational entropy along the integration path. They applied Atomic Cluster Expansion (ACE) potentials trained on DFT data using LDA, PBE, and r2SCAN functionals to the Au-Cu binary alloy system.
AI.result: Explicit configurational sampling lowered the AuCu order-disorder transition temperature predicted by the LDA-trained ACE potential from ~810 K to 710 K (closer to experimental 683 K) and substantially widened the stability range of the solid solution. However, significant sensitivity to the exchange-correlation functional was observed, indicating that this level of agreement should not be interpreted as general predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Non-ideal configurational entropy must be sampled explicitly, alongside a careful choice of functional, for a reliable atomistic description of binary phase diagrams. The study demonstrates both the importance of configurational entropy sampling and the need for careful functional selection in predictive materials modeling.

title: Derivation and application of a general scaling relation between the dc and asymptotic high-frequency optical Hall responses
summary: Based on the Kramers-Krong relations, we derive and apply a quite general expression that that relates the low frequency quasi-dc Hall conductivity to the asymptotic high frequency optical Hall response (and its manifestations in Kerr and Faraday effects) for time-reversal symmetry breaking (TRSB) states of matter like ferromagnets and time-reversal symmetry breaking superconductors as well as metals in magnetic field. Parametric plots shows this relation is obeyed exactly for the trivial single-mode case of sharp cyclotron resonance, and approximately for theoretical models for ferromagnets and TRSB superconductors. We also apply it to the experimental data from a variety of ferromagnetic systems and show reasonable agreement there as well. We use the relation to predict, from the size of the spontaneous Kerr effect at the pseudogap temperature of the cuprate superconductors that cuprates should exhibit an anomalous Hall effect of approximately 0.1 Ohm$^{-1}\cdot$cm$^{-1}$. This is a small value, but one within experimental reach and we encourage the search for it. Although not explicitly quantum geometric, our treatment has some similarities to efforts to set bounds on physical quantities based on quantum geometric relations and limited physical information.

AI.tldr: The paper derives a general expression using Kramers-Kronig relations that connects low-frequency Hall conductivity to high-frequency optical Hall response (Kerr and Faraday effects) for time-reversal symmetry breaking systems, with applications to ferromagnets, TRSB superconductors, and cuprate superconductors.
AI.motivation: To establish a fundamental relation between dc and optical Hall responses in time-reversal symmetry breaking states of matter, enabling predictions of one from the other and providing a tool to search for anomalous Hall effects in materials like cuprate superconductors.
AI.method: Derivation using Kramers-Kronig relations to relate quasi-dc Hall conductivity to asymptotic high-frequency optical Hall response; validated through parametric plots against theoretical models (cyclotron resonance, ferromagnets, TRSB superconductors) and experimental ferromagnetic data.
AI.result: The relation holds exactly for sharp cyclotron resonance and approximately for theoretical models; agrees with experimental ferromagnetic data; predicts an anomalous Hall effect of ~0.1 Ohm^-1·cm^-1 in cuprate superconductors based on spontaneous Kerr effect at pseudogap temperature.
AI.conclusion: This Kramers-Kronig based relation provides a useful tool for predictingdc Hall behavior from optical measurements and vice versa, with the prediction for cuprates being small but experimentally accessible, encouraging experimental verification.

title: SevenNet-Polar for MultiTask Prediction of Energy, Forces, Stress, and Born Effective Charges: Development and Application to ZrO$_2$, Li$_3$PO$_4$, and Perovskites
summary: Accurate prediction of the Born effective charge (BEC) tensor is crucial for modeling materials under electric fields but remains computationally expensive. To bridge this gap, we present SevenNet-Polar, an equivariant graph neural network framework based on the SevenNet architecture for fast and accurate BEC predictions. Our BEC-only predictors can achieve an RMSE as low as 0.0043 e on ZrO$_2$, Li$_3$PO$_4$, and perovskites, despite the presence of high-temperature (up to 2,000 K) and defect-laden training data. Our all-in-one multitask models for predicting energy, forces, stress, and BEC in ZrO$_2$ and Li$_3$PO$_4$ achieve high accuracy with an RMSE of 1.0 meV/atom for energy, 12 meV/angstrom for forces, 0.05 GPa for stress, and 0.0029 e for BEC. BEC accuracy is not degraded by multitask training. Scaling analysis reveals distinct exponents for diagonal and off-diagonal BEC components, both of which exhibit less favorable scaling than energy, force and stress errors. SevenNet-Polar generalizes robustly when tested on scenarios containing structural environments absent from the training set, such as along nudged elastic band (NEB) trajectories or grain boundaries in ZrO$_2$. Accelerated by FlashTP, SevenNet-Polar enables simulations containing up to 1.5 million atoms on multi-GPU supercomputers and up to approximately 15,000 atoms on a single consumer-grade GPU. This makes charge-aware molecular dynamics simulations under electric fields more accessible.

AI.tldr: SevenNet-Polar is an equivariant graph neural network for fast and accurate prediction of Born effective charge (BEC) tensors, enabling large-scale charge-aware molecular dynamics simulations under electric fields.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of Born effective charge (BEC) tensor is crucial for modeling materials under electric fields but remains computationally expensive, creating a need for fast and accurate machine learning predictors.
AI.method: SevenNet-Polar is an equivariant graph neural network framework based on the SevenNet architecture, developed as both BEC-only predictors and all-in-one multitask models for predicting energy, forces, stress, and BEC simultaneously.
AI.result: BEC-only predictors achieve RMSE as low as 0.0043 e on ZrO2, Li3PO4, and perovskites. Multitask models achieve RMSE of 1.0 meV/atom for energy, 12 meV/angstrom for forces, 0.05 GPa for stress, and 0.0029 e for BEC. The model generalizes robustly to unseen structural environments including NEB trajectories and grain boundaries. Enables simulations with up to 1.5 million atoms on multi-GPU systems.
AI.conclusion: SevenNet-Polar makes charge-aware molecular dynamics simulations under electric fields more accessible by enabling large-scale simulations on both supercomputers and consumer-grade GPUs without degrading BEC accuracy through multitask training.

title: Fast two-dimensional tensor-network contraction via subspace iteration
summary: The corner transfer matrix renormalization group (CTMRG) is one of the standard contraction methods for infinite projected entangled-pair states (iPEPS), but its computational cost is dominated by repeated truncated singular value decompositions (SVDs).
